title: Traveling!
text:
My travel destinations


Code
# set up 
Sys.setenv(PROJ_LIB = "/opt/conda/share/proj")




Code
# loading required libraries
library(sf)  # spatial data handling             
library(leaflet) # interactive map creation       
library(htmltools) # HTML rendering for labels and popups       
library(janitor) # cleaning column names
library(tidyverse) # data manipulation and piping
library(readr)  # reading CSV files




Code
# load map.csv and clean column names to lowercase
invisible(Travel <- read_csv("map.csv") %>% clean_names())

# build interactive map from travel data
leaflet(data = Travel) %>%
  addProviderTiles("Esri.WorldImagery") %>% # satellite imagery background
  
  # clickable markers that show country name in popup
  addMarkers(~long, ~latitude, popup = ~country) %>%
  
  # always-visible labels below each marker
  addLabelOnlyMarkers(
    ~long, ~latitude,
    label = ~country, # change ~place to ~country
    labelOptions = labelOptions(
      noHide = TRUE,  # label always visible, not just on hover
      direction = "bottom", # label sits below the marker pin
      textOnly = TRUE, # no background box, just text 
      style = list( 
        "color" = "white",  # white text for visibility on satellite map
        "font-size" = "12px", # change font size
        "font-weight" = "bold" # bold text for clarity
      )
    )
  )

text: Hi, I hope you are having a wonderful day! My name is Bella Kormos, and I am a third year student at University of California, Santa Barbara. I love traveling, being in nature, playing volleyball, doing yoga, wheel-throwing, and backpacking.

title: Built Environment: The East Oakland Compost Center Project Proposal
text:
The EOCC is a community based initiative to help address social inequity, food insecurity, and community wellness through composting and local empowerment. In the pdf below, I have attached my final project for my class, ENVS 155: The built environment, where I wrote a proposal as a potential solution to the food injustices in my hometown of Oakland.
ENVS 155 Final Project

title: Environmental Statistics: Midterm
text:
In the code and text below, I explore and analyze the difference in mean tussock diameter across burned vs un-burned sites in the moist acidic tussock tundra at the site of the 2007 Anaktuvuk River fire. This is problem 3 from my midterm linked below, and I explain the variables, hypotheses, run appropriate statistical tests, and ultimatley visualize the data.



The response variable: Average tussock diameter measured in cm, which is a quantitative, continuous variable.
The predictor variable is the site type (burned or unburned) within the 2007 Anatuvuk River Fire scar, which is a binary categorical variable.
The response variable measurement: The researchers measured the average tussock diameter along four 100 meter transects that were located in each cardinal direction from the meteorological tower. The tussocks that intercepted the transect tape were measured in two directions (N-S and E-W) with a caliper (Methods section, paragraph 5 (Microtopography)).





The null hypothesis (H0): There is no significant difference in the mean tussock mound diameters (cm) in burned and unburned sites.
The alternative Hypothesis (HA): There is a significant difference in the mean tussock mound diameters (cm) in burned and unburned sites.






Code
library(janitor)
library(rstatix) # new package for effect size
library(tidyverse) # load in all packages library(janitor)
library(flextable) # new package to create a table library(rstatix) # new package for effect size

tussocks <- read_csv("tussock_height.csv") # store the data and save object as "tussocks" temp <- read_csv("temp_2024.csv") # store the data and save object as "temp"







Code
tussocks_clean <- tussocks |> # start with tussocks data, create new object with clean data
  clean_names() |> # clean column names (lower case with underscores)
  select(site, average_diameter) |> # select only the site and average_diameter columns
  mutate(site = case_when(
    site == "Sev" ~ "Burned", # change "Sev" site name to "Burned"
    site == "Unb" ~ "Unburned")) # change "Unb" site name to "Unburned"
tussocks_clean |>
slice_sample(n = 10) # displays 10 random rows from the "tussocks_clean" data-frame


# A tibble: 10 × 2
   site     average_diameter
   <chr>               <dbl>
 1 Unburned             31.5
 2 Unburned             13.6
 3 Unburned             20  
 4 Unburned             25.5
 5 Unburned             18.9
 6 Burned               24.3
 7 Burned               24.6
 8 Unburned             19.9
 9 Unburned             20.6
10 Unburned             20.5







Code
tussocks_summary <- tussocks_clean |> # create an object called tussocks_summary using the tussocks_clean data frame
  group_by(site) |> # group by site 
  summarize(
    mean = round(mean(average_diameter, na.rm = TRUE), digits = 1), # calculates the mean and rounds the value to 1 decimal place
    se = round(sd(average_diameter, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()), digits = 1) # calculates standard error and rounds the value to 1 decimal place
)
# create polished table using flextable
tussocks_summary |> # pipe the tussocks_summary data frame into the table
  flextable() |> # flextable() converts the data frame into a formatted table object # renames the column headers to be more readable
  set_header_labels(site = "Site",
                    mean = "Mean",
                    se = "Standard Error") |>
  autofit() # automatically adjusts column widths to fit the content properly                       


SiteMeanStandard ErrorBurned23.80.7Unburned19.70.6





Check 1: Visual tool - Normally distributed variable


Code
ggplot(data = tussocks_clean,
  aes(sample = average_diameter)) + # specifies the variable to check for normality
geom_qq_line(color = "darkgreen") + # making reference line green so its easier to see
  geom_qq() + # geom_qq() plots the QQ points comparing data to theoretical normal distribution
  facet_wrap(~site) + # creates separate plots for each site (Burned and Unburned)
  labs(title = "QQ Plot for Tussock Diameter by Site") + # adds title to the plot
  theme_light() # applies a minimal theme to the plot









Visual check: The points on the QQ plot for both burned and unburned sites approximately follow the reference line with only minor deviations at the tails. The reference line represents the theoretical distribution, so tussock diameters appear normally distributed.
Check 2: Equal Variance Test


Code
var.test( # perform an F-test to compare variances in tussock diameters
  average_diameter ~ site, # test whether variances of tussock diameters differ by site 
  data = tussocks_clean) # use the cleaned tussock data-set



    F test to compare two variances

data:  average_diameter by site
F = 0.92558, num df = 78, denom df = 110, p-value = 0.7221
alternative hypothesis: true ratio of variances is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.6169823 1.4090774
sample estimates:
ratio of variances 
         0.9255843 


Using a F-test of equal variance, I determined that group variances are equal (F-test, F(78, 110) = 0.93, p = 0.72).





Code
t.test( # perform a two-sample t-test to compare mean tussock diameter
  average_diameter ~ site, # test whether mean tussock diameters differ by site
  var.equal = TRUE, # assume equal variances based on the variance test
  data = tussocks_clean # use the cleaned data frame again
)



    Two Sample t-test

data:  average_diameter by site
t = 4.4663, df = 188, p-value = 1.371e-05
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Burned and group Unburned is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 2.298075 5.934027
sample estimates:
  mean in group Burned mean in group Unburned 
              23.78632               19.67027 





We found a significant difference in tussock mound diameters between unburned and burned sites (Student’s t-test: t(188) = 4.5, p < 0.001, α = 0.05) with a moderate effect size (Cohen’s d = 0.66). On average, the mean tussock diameters were larger by about 4.1 cm in burned sites (mean = 23.8 cm, SE = 0.7, n = 79) in comparison to unburned sites (mean = 19.7 cm, SE = 0.6, n = 111).





Code
# create plot with x-axis as site, y-axis as average diameter
ggplot(data = tussocks_clean,
       aes(x = site,
           y = average_diameter,
           color = site,
           shape = site)) +
# adds individual data points
geom_jitter(width = 0.2, # jitters points horizontally
            height = 0, # prevents vertical jitter
            alpha = 0.4, # make transparent
            size = 2) + # edit site of points
stat_summary(geom = "point", # adds mean as a point
             fun = mean, # calculates mean for each site
             color = "red", # makes mean point "red"
             size = 3) + # makes mean point larger than data points
stat_summary(fun.data = mean_se, # calculates standard error and mean geom = "errorbar", # displays error bars
             width = 0.2, # sets width of error bar caps
             linewidth = 0.8, # sets thickness of error bar lines
             color = "red") + # colors error bars red
# adds plot title, author name as a subtitle, and labels axes
labs(title = "Tussock Mound Diameter by Site Type",
     subtitle = "Bella Kormos",
     x = "Site",
     y = "Average diameter (cm)") +
# use a theme that creates white background without gridlines
theme_classic() + # removes legend
theme(legend.position = "none") +
# sets custom colors for each site
scale_color_manual(values = c("Burned" = "forestgreen", "Unburned" = "lightpink")) +
# sets custom shapes (5 = open diamond, 0 = square)
scale_shape_manual(values = c("Burned" = 5, "Unburned" = 0))












Figure 1. Mean tussock diameter differs across sites. The green open diamonds represent tussock diameters measured (cm) at burned sites, and the pink open squares represent the tussock diameter measured (cm) at unburned sites. The red points represent the means for each site, and lines above and below represent the standard error bars. Data Source: Rocha, A. 2021. Tussock height and diameter in moist acidic tussock tundra at the site of the 2007 Anaktuvuk River fire scar, and nearby unburned tundra measured in 2016 ver 1. Environmental Data Initiative. https://doi.org/10.6073/ pasta/1dccd3fdb3aa693f9c2b69a24f8306ed (Accessed 2026-02-10).

title: Environmental Statistics: Midterm
text:
In the code and text below, I explore and analyze the difference in mean tussock diameter across burned vs un-burned sites in the moist acidic tussock tundra at the site of the 2007 Anaktuvuk River fire. The alternative Hypothesis (HA): There is a significant difference in the mean tussock mound diameters (cm) in burned and unburned sites.

Code

  select(site, average_diameter) |> # select only the site and average_diameter columns

    site == "Sev" ~ "Burned", # change "Sev" site name to "Burned"
    site == "Unb" ~ "Unburned")) # change "Unb" site name to "Unburned"
tussocks_clean |>

 1 Unburned             31.5

 5 Unburned             18.9

10 Unburned             20.5

Code

  aes(sample = average_diameter)) + # specifies the variable to check for normality
geom_qq_line(color = "darkgreen") + # making reference line green so its easier to see
  geom_qq() + # geom_qq() plots the QQ points comparing data to theoretical normal distribution
  facet_wrap(~site) + # creates separate plots for each site (Burned and Unburned)
  labs(title = "QQ Plot for Tussock Diameter by Site") + # adds title to the plot
  theme_light() # applies a minimal theme to the plot

  average_diameter ~ site, # test whether variances of tussock diameters differ by site 
  data = tussocks_clean) # use the cleaned tussock data-set

data:  average_diameter by site
F = 0.92558, num df = 78, denom df = 110, p-value = 0.7221
alternative hypothesis: true ratio of variances is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.6169823 1.4090774
sample estimates:
ratio of variances 
         0.9255843

# create plot with x-axis as site, y-axis as average diameter
ggplot(data = tussocks_clean,
       aes(x = site,
           y = average_diameter,

# adds individual data points

            size = 2) + # edit site of points

     subtitle = "Bella Kormos",
     x = "Site",

# use a theme that creates white background without gridlines

theme(legend.position = "none") +
# sets custom colors for each site

# sets custom shapes (5 = open diamond, 0 = square)

title: Backpacking & Hiking
section: Bulgaria Rewilding in Action: Wildland’s Field Study Program
text:
Bulgaria Rewilding in Action: Wildland’s Field Study Program

    s


2024: While backpacking, I studied the coexistence of large carnivores and humans in the Bulgarian mountains.

title: Backpacking & Hiking
text:
Glacier & Olympic National Park

  


2023: Road trip to Oregon, Washington, and Montana.

title: About me!
text:
Hometown
I am from the Bay Area in the beautiful Oakland hills! I grew up with my sister, my parents, my Baba, and my dog! My family and I love going to the beach, tide-pooling, and traveling.

     



My happiest place
Journaling in the forest near my house is my happiest place. I also absolutley love discovering new cafés, doing yoga, and playing volleyball!

 



I am half-Bulgarian
And I grew up hiking in the Bulgarian mountains and speaking the language! Visiting my family and the beautiful country was a big reason that I decided to study the environment.

  



Fun fact
I lived in Rabat, Morocco for a year when I was younger!

 



Madrid!
I studied abroad in Madrid, and I have been studying spanish since the 6th grade. Madrid was a magical place to live and I hope to live in Spain again if I get the chance!
